what_it_does: The shown portion defines the Gallery web page shell, metadata, shared surface stylesheet imports, and a large embedded CSS layer for a design-review gallery with a sidebar registry rail, stage iframe viewer, comparison controls, feedback pins, summary modal, token drawer, grammar drawer, layouts drawer, and mobile-specific work/panel behavior.
observations: Declares the page as an installable/mobile-capable app with manifest, Apple touch icon, theme color hook, and Google font imports.
Imports generated shared surface CSS and room CSS before a large file-local style block.
Uses CSS custom properties heavily for color, spacing, radius, type, motion, and surface semantics.
Defines a left sidebar rail with searchable categories, groups, item cards, thumbnails, pending ghost items, and active states.
Defines a main stage with topbar identity, version switching, polarity toggle, review/open controls, comparison bar, iframe frames, navigation arrows, and an info strip.
Includes comment overlay styles for arming, click catcher, numbered pins, popovers, inline comment input, and deletion controls.
Includes a feedback strip with verdict buttons, note input, and saved status text.
Defines three off-canvas drawers: token controls on the right, grammar controls on the left, and layout archetype controls on the right.
Token and grammar drawers use namespaced class and variable layers; the layouts drawer uses the shared surface variables directly.

Contains extensive mobile media-query behavior that turns the sidebar into a sheet, makes the viewer a work/panel split, hides or summons review controls, and changes stage scrolling and layout behavior.
Comments carry design rationale, mobile issue references, and implementation intent alongside the CSS.
standouts: The page is structured as a review instrument rather than a static gallery: viewing, comparing, annotating, adjusting tokens, inspecting grammar, and previewing layouts are all represented in the local styles.
The CSS treats generated design tokens as the primary substrate, with many controls described as addresses into a shared surface layer.
The mobile layer is detailed and opinionated, including sticky identity, hidden review controls, off-canvas rail, split viewer behavior, and draggable-joint assumptions.
conventions: Uses semantic surface tokens instead of hardcoded palette values for most visual styling.
Uses short, namespace-like class prefixes for drawer systems such as tk, gm, and ly.
Uses state classes such as .open, .show, .active, .on, .closed, .ghost, and .arming.
Keeps page-specific styling inline in the HTML rather than moving it to a separate gallery stylesheet.
Uses comments as architectural annotations and change-log notes inside CSS.
Relies on fixed internal preview dimensions such as 1600x900 and 1280x720, then scales or frames them through CSS hooks.
concerns: The embedded style block is very large for an HTML file and mixes page structure, component styling, state styling, drawer systems, and mobile behavior in one place.
Several selectors are redefined later in the same style block, including .item, .item:hover, .item.active, and .item h3, which makes final behavior depend on order.
The file-local CSS encodes many implementation assumptions about JavaScript-managed states and measurements that are not visible in the shown portion.
Long rationale comments improve traceability but make the stylesheet dense to scan and maintain.
Multiple fixed widths and viewport-sized panels may require continual testing across narrow and unusual viewport sizes.
notes: This file is the Gallery page surface: a token-driven review workspace with rail navigation, iframe stage, annotation/review controls, and design-system drawers.
questions: Where are the registry data, iframe sources, drawer state, pin persistence, and mobile split measurements initialized?
Is the duplicate .item styling intentional as an override layer or leftover from an earlier rail card design?
Does the empty theme-color meta tag always get populated before paint, or can mobile browser chrome briefly see an empty value?
purpose_vs_actual: The stated gallery/instrument purpose matches the shown content: the file builds a gallery/review surface and exposes controls for inspecting and adjusting design substrates.
apparent_themes: Design review as an interactive workspace
Generated surface variables as shared substrate
intention_signals: The file seems intended to make the gallery a live instrument for reviewing design pieces while exposing the underlying token, grammar, and layout systems without leaving the page.
novelty: The unusual aspect is the combination of gallery viewer, annotation layer, token editor, generative grammar preview, and layout archetype inspector inside one token-driven HTML surface.
contribution: This file contributes a concrete gallery/review shell that a fused system would keep as an example of how design substrates can be browsed, compared, annotated, and adjusted through one shared surface vocabulary.
summary_for_embedding: A token-driven Gallery HTML page defining metadata and extensive CSS for a design-review workspace with sidebar navigation, iframe stage, compare/review controls, comment pins, summary modal, token drawer, grammar drawer, layouts drawer, and mobile work/panel behavior.
intention_for_embedding: This file was for turning design DNA assets into an inspectable, adjustable gallery instrument where pieces, versions, annotations, tokens, grammar, and layouts can be reviewed together.
